topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official email sat accusingly on my screen, the subject line stark and unforgiving. Dr. Alistair Vance was departing. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I reread the message three times, the words blurring slightly at the edges. It wasn't just a departure; it was the abrupt severing of the lifeline I had been clinging to for the last eighteen months. I stood up, pacing the cramped confines of my dorm room, the cheap carpet muffling the sound of my hurried footsteps. Each step seemed heavy, weighted by the sudden emptiness where his steady guidance used to be. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and stared blankly at the blank page of my thesis proposal. How was I supposed to navigate the next phase without his critical eye? A wave of sheer panic washed over me, making my palms sweat.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at the clock on the wall, the second hand ticking with maddening slowness. Every time it moved, it felt like another minute stolen from the potential of my research. When the news finally hit me—a hushed conversation in the library café—my hands began to shake, a fine tremor running up my forearms. I felt a distinct tightness in my chest, as if someone had clamped a hand over my lungs. I retreated to the deserted quad, leaning against the rough brick of an old building. The autumn air felt sharp and unforgiving against my skin. I watched students laughing, their conversations flowing easily, and felt intensely isolated, a ship adrift without a compass. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force a calm breath, but it caught in my throat, ragged and shallow.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the office seemed too bright, exposing every flaw in my current work. When Professor Vance announced his resignation, the sound of his voice, usually so measured and reassuring, sounded brittle, like old glass about to shatter. I sat motionless in my chair, my gaze fixed on the framed photo of him on the shelf—a moment of shared intellectual triumph frozen in time. A profound sense of disorientation settled over me. My thoughts became a chaotic loop: what would happen to the timeline? Who would review the methodology? I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal on linoleum echoing loudly in the quiet room. I walked to the window, looking out at the sprawling campus, feeling an overwhelming sense of vulnerability, as if the foundations of my entire academic structure had just dissolved.
